title: Add Two Numbers
code: public ListNode AddTwoNumbers(ListNode l1, ListNode l2)

frames.caption: Set up a dummy head and a tail cursor; carry starts at 0. We will walk both reversed lists in lockstep, adding one column at a time.
frames.note: The lists store digits least-significant-first, so their heads line up as the ones column. A dummy head lets the append loop stay branch-free, and a single carry variable threads the whole addition together.

frames.caption: Column 0: a=2, b=5, carry=0 → sum = 2 + 5 + 0 = 7.
frames.note: Reversed storage means the heads are the least-significant digits, so we add column by column LEFT to RIGHT exactly like grade-school addition: sum = a + b + carry. The new digit is sum % 10 (what stays in this column) and the new carry is sum / 10 (what spills into the next, more-significant column). Because both lists are already reversed, no extra reversing or stacks are needed — one forward pass does it.

frames.caption: Emit digit 7 (sum % 10) and carry 0 (sum / 10). Append node 7 to the result, advance tail and both cursors.
frames.note: Append the digit as a fresh node at the tail of the result list, then advance tail and both input cursors. A DUMMY head node makes this loop clean: tail always points at a real node, so tail.next = newNode never special-cases an empty list — at the end we just return dummy.next.

frames.caption: Column 1: a=4, b=6, carry=0 → sum = 4 + 6 + 0 = 10.
frames.note: Reversed storage means the heads are the least-significant digits, so we add column by column LEFT to RIGHT exactly like grade-school addition: sum = a + b + carry. The new digit is sum % 10 (what stays in this column) and the new carry is sum / 10 (what spills into the next, more-significant column). Because both lists are already reversed, no extra reversing or stacks are needed — one forward pass does it.

frames.caption: Emit digit 0 (sum % 10) and carry 1 (sum / 10). Append node 0 to the result, advance tail and both cursors.
frames.note: Append the digit as a fresh node at the tail of the result list, then advance tail and both input cursors. A DUMMY head node makes this loop clean: tail always points at a real node, so tail.next = newNode never special-cases an empty list — at the end we just return dummy.next.

frames.caption: Column 2: a=3, b=4, carry=1 → sum = 3 + 4 + 1 = 8.
frames.note: Reversed storage means the heads are the least-significant digits, so we add column by column LEFT to RIGHT exactly like grade-school addition: sum = a + b + carry. The new digit is sum % 10 (what stays in this column) and the new carry is sum / 10 (what spills into the next, more-significant column). Because both lists are already reversed, no extra reversing or stacks are needed — one forward pass does it.

frames.caption: Emit digit 8 (sum % 10) and carry 0 (sum / 10). Append node 8 to the result, advance tail and both cursors.
frames.note: Append the digit as a fresh node at the tail of the result list, then advance tail and both input cursors. A DUMMY head node makes this loop clean: tail always points at a real node, so tail.next = newNode never special-cases an empty list — at the end we just return dummy.next.

frames.caption: Both lists exhausted and carry is 0 → stop. Result reads 7 → 0 → 8 = 807 (807 = 342 + 465). Return dummy.next.
frames.note: Keep looping while EITHER list still has nodes OR carry != 0. A missing digit counts as 0 (the l1?.val ?? 0 pattern), and a leftover final carry forces one more node — that is how 5 + 5 produces 0 then a trailing 1. Time O(max(n, m)), space O(max(n, m)) for the result list.
